scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: All key entry points are named with file paths: chat_completion.rb (with a line ref), function_dispatch.rb, function_tool_adapter.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb, and the full mcp/ subtree — the agent can pick any subsystem and make one targeted read, not a broad search. The supporting files (configuration.rb for max_tool_calls, completion_context.rb for hooks) also appear, giving the agent the full peripheral map.

specificity.rationale: Paths are concrete and module names are exact (ChatCompletion, FunctionDispatch, FunctionToolAdapter) with two line-level anchors (lib/raix.rb:23 for Zeitwerk, chat_completion.rb:340 for ruby_llm_request); the only gap is that paths are relative (lib/raix/…) rather than absolute, but the agent can resolve those in one lookup.

justification.rationale: The answer states code is "mixed into host classes as ActiveSupport::Concern modules" but never explains WHY — that a host class includes only the concerns it needs (a la carte composition) so chat, function dispatch, and MCP are independently optional; the design motivation is absent, leaving the agent to infer it.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere — no 'verify this' markers for how the concerns compose at runtime, how max_tool_calls interacts with the loop, or where completion_context.rb hooks fire; the agent is given an unqualified map with no signal about what to confirm before acting.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces every layer with exact file:line — entry at chat_completion.rb:73, tool build at :91-97, dispatch at :180-187 → function_dispatch.rb:80-101, transcript re-read at :195-196, recursion at :198-209, and short-circuit at :210-218 — giving the agent a complete path to intercept any layer. The only minor gap is that `ruby_llm_request` at :340 and `FunctionToolAdapter` at :382-385 are cited but their exact role in the first request isn't tied back to the round-trip path.

specificity.rationale: Named exactly: `chat_completion`, `ruby_llm_request`, `dispatch_tool_function`, `stop_tool_calls_and_respond!`, `max_tool_calls`, `FunctionToolAdapter`, `function_dispatch.rb:80-101` for transcript append — every piece has a file:line reference rather than a vague description.

justification.rationale: The answer describes what happens (append to transcript, recurse with decremented max_tool_calls) but never explains WHY the loop auto-continues — i.e., that the model's tool-call response is not a final answer, so the framework must feed results back and re-invoke the model until it returns plain text. The mechanism is listed, not reasoned.

uncertainty.rationale: The three branch points (max_tool_calls cutoff at :165-177, stop_tool_calls_and_respond! at :210-218, plain-text termination at :221-235) are all identified, but the answer presents them as settled fact with no flag to the agent to verify the recursion condition or what happens when multiple tool calls arrive in one response.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: Traces the full pipeline with file:line at every hop: declaration at function_dispatch.rb:46, storage in @functions :47-65, tools wrapper :126-130, FunctionToolAdapter conversion at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57/:6-48, invocation site at chat_completion.rb:382-385, and filtering at :91-97/:303-313 — an agent can act on this without re-exploring.

specificity.rationale: Every named piece (FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm, filtered_tools, UndeclaredToolError, available_tools) is tied to a concrete file:line; nothing is left as vague prose like 'the function code'.

justification.rationale: The answer names what each part does but only hints at the WHY: 'OpenAI shape' and 'strict/additionalProperties:false defaults' give partial rationale, but the design motivation — that a host class declares tools as a class-level DSL so behavior is added declaratively — is not stated.

uncertainty.rationale: UndeclaredToolError implicitly flags a filtering gotcha, and the strict-schema defaults note warns about schema shape, but there are no explicit 'verify this' signals for the areas most likely to trip an agent (parameter schema format, required vs optional handling at :54-63, naming collisions).

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three key adapters are named with file:line and tied to specific chat_completion.rb call sites (e.g., ':133,139' for Base, ':358' for multimodal, ':349-372' for the replay loop); an agent could pick up any adapter and start working with only small targeted lookups. specificity.rationale: Concrete file names (transcript_adapter.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb) with granular line ranges (':63-80', ':82-97', ':51-61') are given for every major method; this is well above 'the message code' vagueness.

justification.rationale: The answer describes what each adapter does but the WHY is only implicit: 'Anthropic cache-control multipart' hints at provider-shape normalization and 'OpenAI image_url content arrays' hints at multimodal normalization, but neither rationale is stated explicitly, leaving the agent to infer purpose rather than understand it.

uncertainty.rationale: No explicit 'verify this' flags are raised; conditional paths (Anthropic vs other providers, multimodal vs plain text) are mentioned in passing but not called out as branching points the agent should confirm before acting.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: Covers all four pieces the rubric asks for with file:line: MCP::Tool (mcp/tool.rb:29-35), both transport clients (mcp/sse_client.rb:32-43/47-77, mcp/stdio_client.rb), the join mechanism (mcp.rb:70-162 including FunctionDispatch inclusion at :96 and function() registration at :108-117), and the unchanged chat loop dispatch (chat_completion.rb:184-186); an agent could open any of these and start editing immediately.

specificity.rationale: Every named component is tied to a concrete file and line range (mcp/tool.rb, mcp.rb, mcp/sse_client.rb, mcp/stdio_client.rb, chat_completion.rb) with no vague references like 'the MCP code'; coerce_arguments/:168-250 is also pinned.

justification.rationale: The answer explains the shared-dispatch mechanism clearly — includes FunctionDispatch, calls function() per tool, define_method proxies to client.call_tool, 'Because it's a normal function, the chat loop … handles it unchanged' — making the architectural intent legible, not just a file list; it stops short of explaining why MCP was designed this way (e.g., to avoid a second dispatch path), so the WHY is partially implied.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer mentions coerce_arguments as an MCP-specific step but does not flag where the agent should verify edge cases (schema format differences between MCP and native declarations, transport error propagation, whether only/except filtering affects schema sent to the model); it reads as fully confident throughout, leaving the agent to discover those divergences on its own.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: All six areas from the rubric are covered with file:line throughout — chat loop line ranges, function DSL, adapter files, MCP transport files, and a full spec matrix including mcp_server.rb support file; an agent could open a PR from this list with only minor targeted follow-ups.

specificity.rationale: Every area carries concrete line numbers (e.g., `dispatch+authorize :180-187`, `function_tool_adapter.rb:6-57`, `transcript_adapter.rb :85-91,:110`) and all four MCP files are named individually; nothing is left at the 'tool code' level of vagueness.

justification.rationale: The answer organizes by category labels ('Chat loop', 'Declaration/schema', etc.) which implies why each group is present, but it does not explain WHY adapters shape the contract, WHY MCP tools share the same dispatch path as local functions, or WHY each spec group would break — it is a well-labeled list, not a reasoned one.

uncertainty.rationale: No hedges appear about what static reading misses: mixin reach is only valid when included in a host class, MCP transport paths differ at runtime, fixture files may need updating, and dynamic dispatch could route through unlisted sites; the answer presents the list with full confidence and no verification prompts.

map_quality.rationale: The JSON edit map covers all four pipeline areas with file:line throughout — chat loop at chat_completion.rb:91-97/160-177/382-385, function DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46-107, schema at function_tool_adapter.rb:6-57, adapters at transcript_adapter.rb:85-91/99-119, and the full MCP stack with mcp.rb:70-162/168-250 plus all four MCP files; specs are enumerated by file.
